entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Students read Norse mythology for a term, enjoy a term of fairy tales from around the world collected or written in the 19th century, and finish with a term of African American folktales.

This topic is included in the following course(s): Literature: Grade 4

About Literature: Grade 4
Overview note: Literature selections are chosen to encourage students to engage with rich ideas in literature and history and also to help them to gain independence in their reading and lessons.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
About Literature: Grade 4
Overview note: Literature selections are chosen to encourage students to engage with rich ideas in literature and history and also to help them to gain independence in their reading and lessons.

Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4
Can easily be combined with students in Grades 3 or 5.

rows.label: Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4
rows.books: In the Days of Giants||Russian Fairy Tales||South American Jungle Tales||The Tales of Uncle Remus: The Adventures of Brer Rabbit||Short Stories & Folktales

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4
In the Days of Giants tells the stories of Norse mythology and is read in Terms 1-2. The Nordic people would enjoy telling tales of heroes, gods, and monsters during the long, dark winter nights. As such, we recommend teachers preview the readings to ensure they are suitable for their particular students.

books.title: The Tales of Uncle Remus: The Adventures of Brer Rabbit

blocks.text:
• Read through the lesson and gather any materials or find any necessary links. 

• If you have not already read the book, you may want to read the selected pages in advance, especially for Shakespeare lessons. It is helpful to read the play or a preview of it beforehand.

• Choose which books to have students start reading independently (General Literature selections are especially good for independent work).

• Connect back to the previous lesson. Ask students to give a summary of what they remember and/or predict what might come next. (Full narration is not necessary.)

• If students have difficulty recalling, teachers might try showing them an illustration, sharing something that the teacher remembers, or prompting them with the first sentence of the last reading. This will often refresh their memory enough to allow them to recall.

• If students still cannot recall anything from the last reading, then teachers might consider continuing anyway (with a shorter reading and/or using a combination of visual and audio support this time) or repeating the last lesson, depending on the reading and the nature of the book.

• Use a picture, map, or engaging question to pique the student's interest in the day's lesson. 

• If necessary, you may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding.

• Read the day's passage aloud or have students read independently. For Shakespeare, it is highly recommended that each student have their own copy of the play and that you read it aloud together or read along and underline/make notes while listening to the audio recording.

• Use supportive strategies and educational tools to reduce frustration and better engage the mind, as appropriate. These could include, but are not limited to, the use of eBooks, pictures, audio, read-aloud, buddy reading, colored reading strips, etc.

• Some students may benefit from having a notebook or piece of scrap paper available to externalize ideas by drawing, recording names, or making symbolic 'squiggle' notes.

• Students who have a difficult time sitting still or listening may benefit from: a fidget of their choice, such as a piece of putty or clay or an afghan; flexible positioning, such as a wobble seat or inverted sitting; or sensory modulation, such as a weighted vest or blanket or adjustment of light, temperature, or sound.

• Have students retell the passage orally or in writing. If writing, encourage students to use the full remaining lesson time and include as much detail as they can remember. For Shakespeare or challenging books, it may be helpful to narrate more frequently and/or use props.

• Alternatively, learners may use words, pictures, legos, dolls, etc. to process and convey ideas in the manner most natural to them.  

• Students who used paper or a notebook to externalize may refer to these for support. Other students who need external support may benefit from looking at a map or picture from the book while narrating.

• Students who tend to use the same type of narration all the time, might be encouraged to try another form of narration by first doing it together through play or discussion.

• Teachers may take turns to model.

• Narration is a complex skill that requires coordination of several parts of the brain. If a student struggles to narrate in general, with a specific subject or book, or on one day but not others, then this is an opportunity to learn. Observe when they struggle with this or any other activity today; notice facial expressions and body language; listen to what they say about why it is hard or how they feel about it. Take note of these observations for later, tell them that you see how hard they are working, and then offer whatever support they need to end on a positive note or take a break for the day, if necessary.

• Give the student an opportunity to share thoughts on the lesson and any connections made.  

• To stimulate discussion, you can ask an open-ended question or use an included discussion question.

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: In the Days of Giants, Student World Atlas

➜ INTRO
We will be reading Norse mythology. It is a series of beliefs and legends belonging to Scandinavian people who lived in modern-day Norway, Sweden, and Denmark. The Nordic people would enjoy telling tales of heroes, gods, and monsters during the long, dark winter nights.

➜ LOCATE
Find Scandinavian countries of today: Denmark, Norway, Sweden
⍞ Map Resource: Student World Atlas

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
The Beginning of Things and How Odin Lost His Eye p.9-16

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Arrow Over the Door OR Seesaw Girl, US Atlas

➜ INTRO & MAPWORK (Option 1: The Arrow Over the Door)
Our story this term takes place during the revolutionary war in what is now the state of New York and is based on a real event that happened. Use your atlas (or look online) and locate the Hudson River and city of Saratoga. Find where you live as well.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.1 p.1-10

• Make a chart of or describe the different groups that are part of the Revolutionary War. 
---------------
➜ INTRO & MAPWORK (Option 2: Seesaw Girl)
Locate Korea on your atlas/globe. Our new story this term takes place during the year 1654 or so in Korea!

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.1-2 p.1-10
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS (Option 2)
View a Korean ceremonial robe from the Joseon period. 
∞ Image Link: Ceremonial Robe

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: The Arrow Over the Door OR Seesaw Girl

➜ MAPWORK (Option 1: The Arrow Over the Door)
Locate Montreal, Rivière-Saint-François, and Odanak in Google Maps and/or your atlas. We'll meet some new characters who live here in our story today.

➜ RECAP, READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Ch.2 p.11-20
---------------
➜ RECAP, READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS (Option 2: Seesaw Girl)
Ch.3-4 p.11-22

➜ VIEW
∞ Image Link: Puzzle ball (possibly like the one Jade had!)
lessons.teacherNotes:
• TERMINOLOGY (Option 1)
In order to give a flavor for the different languages the different people in the book are speaking, the author uses the name "Bostoniak" in this chapter. In English they would be called Bostonians – people from Boston.

• ILLUSTRATE
Draw or paint a scene from the story this week.

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus

PREP: For history and context on this collection of Brer Rabbit tales and their broader relevance, read Julius Lester's Foreword on p.xiii-xxi

➜ INTRO
Many different stories were published and told in the 1700s. Some stories which became increasingly popular were about a rabbit. A rabbit is the main character in many folktales from Africa and as more and more people in Africa were enslaved and brought to the American colonies, "they took stories from home about a hare – Wakaima – and adapted them to their new surroundings" (viii). Overtime, a new set of stories developed about a rabbit, now named Brer Rabbit, through this interaction of cultures from Africa, America, and the West Indies. The stories were told aloud and changed depending on the storyteller and the listener, so while they are old stories they are also new every time they are told.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.4-10 "How Brer Fox and Brer Dog Became Enemies," “Hold’im Down, Brer Fox," "Brer Rabbit Comes to Dinner"
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ TEACHER NOTE
There is an audiobook version of these tales read by the author which is a great option to give students a bit more independence and allow them to experiece the stories orally. Note that only selections of the book are included in the lesson plans. Some of the ommitted stories have content you might want to preview before allowing students to listen the whole book.

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus

➜ INTRO
Who would you expect to win a contest or a fight between a rabbit and a fox?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.16-23 "Brer Rabbit Gets Even"

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 27
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.26-31 "Brer Turtle, Brer Rabbit, and Brer Fox" and "Brer Wolf Tries to Catch Brer Rabbit"

• Were you worried Brer Rabbit might not get away this time?

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus and painting/drawing supplies

➜ INTRO
If you have heard it before, tell what you remember about the story of the turtle racing the hare. Today's story is a different version of it!

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.31-34 "Brer Rabbit Finally Gets Beaten"

• What did you think of the story?
• How was Brer Turtle the perfect match for Brer Rabbit?

➜ ILLUSTRATE
Draw or paint an illustration to go with today's story!

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 29
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.42-43 "Brer Fox and Brer Rabbit Go Hunting"
p.46-50 "Brer Rabbit Eats the Butter"

• What do you think Brer Fox meant when he said he "caught a handful of
  common sense"?
• What do you think about the ending of the story with Brer Rabbit and 
  the butter? Did it surprise you? If you made up a different ending for
  the story, what would it be?

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus

➜ INTRO
What do you think it means if I say someone is "resourceful"?

➜ READ
p.54-56 "Brer Rabbit's Children"
p.60-62 "Brer Rabbit and Brer Lion"

➜ NARRATE & DISCUSS
Since these readings are shorter, practice giving as much detail as possible in your written or oral narrations. Tell/write the stories as if you were telling them to a friend who had never heard them before!

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.68-71 "The Talking House" and "Brer Rabbit Gets Eaten Again"
p.96-99 "The Moon in the Pond"

• Have you ever seen the moon in a pond or a puddle?

➜ ILLUSTRATE
With remaining time, illustrate a scene from one of today's stories.

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.99-101 "Why Brer Bear Has No Tail"
p.104-106 "Brer Rabbit Gets the Money"

• If you made up a Brer Rabbit story, what would it be about? Maybe try making one up this week!

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 33
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.113-115 "The Noise in the Woods"
p.122-124 "Brer Rabbit Tells on Brer Wolf"

• What things do you admire or like about Brer Rabbit? What things do you think he does that are wrong?

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus

➜ INTRO
One of our stories today is called "How Brer Rabbit Became a Scary Monster." What do you think is going to happen?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.127-131 "How Brer Rabbit Became a Scary Monster"
p.137-141 "Brer Rabbit Outwits Mr. Man"

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 35
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Tales of Uncle Remus

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on lessons in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings.

lessons.title: 20m Mythology/Hist. Fiction: Grade 4 - Lesson 36
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to course.

Questions will come from:
The Tales of Uncle Remus

terms.content: · In the Days of Giants: Give a short account of one of Odin's or Loki's adventures using words or pictures.

· The Arrow over the Door: Describe your favorite character in the book. 
OR 
Seesaw Girl: Tell about Jade's adventure outside the house.
· Tales of Uncle Remus: Tell the story of Brer Rabbit and Brer Turtle OR A story about Brer Rabbit and Brer Fox.
